1‚Üí---
     2‚Üíname: code-cleaner
     3‚Üídescription: Comprehensively lint, format, and type-check Python code using ruff and ty. Iteratively fixes all linting, formatting, and type issues within a user-defined scope.
     4‚Üí---
     5‚Üí
     6‚Üí# Python Lint & Format
     7‚Üí
     8‚ÜíLint, format, and type-check Python code using `ruff` and `ty` to identify and fix issues across files in a specified scope.
     9‚Üí
    10‚Üí## When to Use
    11‚Üí
    12‚Üí- User asks to lint, format, or clean up Python code
    13‚Üí- User mentions tools like ruff or type checking
    14‚Üí- User requests to "fix all issues" or "clean up" Python files
    15‚Üí- Preparing code for pull requests or CI/CD pipelines
    16‚Üí
    17‚Üí## Instructions
    18‚Üí
    19‚Üí### 1. Clarify Scope
    20‚Üí
    21‚ÜíDetermine:
    22‚Üí- **Scope**: Which files or directories to process?
    23‚Üí- **Fix Level**: Auto-fix issues or just report them?
    24‚Üí- **Type Checking**: Include type checking? (default: yes)
    25‚Üí- **Configuration**: Check for existing config files (pyproject.toml, ruff.toml)
    26‚Üí
    27‚ÜíIf unclear, ask the user before proceeding.
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí### 2. Discover Scope
    30‚Üí
    31‚ÜíIdentify Python files in scope:
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí```bash
    34‚Üífind /path/to/scope -name "*.py" -type f
    35‚Üí```
    36‚Üí
    37‚ÜíReport how many files were found and confirm before proceeding.
    38‚Üí
    39‚Üí### 3. Initial Assessment
    40‚Üí
    41‚ÜíRun tools in check-only mode:
    42‚Üí
    43‚Üí```bash
    44‚Üíruff check /path/to/scope
    45‚Üíruff format --check /path/to/scope
    46‚Üíty /path/to/scope
    47‚Üí```
    48‚Üí
    49‚ÜíSummarize findings: linting issues count, files with formatting issues, type errors count.
    50‚Üí
    51‚Üí### 4. Iterative Fixing Process
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí#### Step A: Format with Ruff
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí```bash
    56‚Üíruff format /path/to/scope
    57‚Üí```
    58‚Üí
    59‚ÜíReport how many files were reformatted.
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí#### Step B: Auto-fix Linting Issues
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üí```bash
    64‚Üíruff check --fix /path/to/scope
    65‚Üí```
    66‚Üí
    67‚Üí#### Step C: Manual Linting Fixes
    68‚Üí
    69‚ÜíFor remaining issues:
    70‚Üí1. Run `ruff check /path/to/scope` to see remaining issues
    71‚Üí2. Group by type and file
    72‚Üí3. Fix manually; explain complex fixes before applying
    73‚Üí4. Re-run `ruff check` after each batch
    74‚Üí5. Iterate until resolved or user confirms to skip
    75‚Üí
    76‚Üí#### Step D: Fix Type Issues
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üí1. Run `ty /path/to/scope` to see type errors
    79‚Üí2. For each error:
    80‚Üí   - **Missing type hints**: Add annotations to functions and variables
    81‚Üí   - **Type mismatches**: Fix incorrect types or add casts
    82‚Üí   - **Missing imports**: Add typing imports (`from typing import ...`)
    83‚Üí   - **Incompatible types**: Refactor to satisfy type constraints
    84‚Üí   - **Any types**: Replace `Any` with specific types where possible
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üí3. Common patterns:
    87‚Üí   ```python
    88‚Üí   def process(data: dict[str, Any]) -> list[str]: ...
    89‚Üí   items: list[int] = []
    90‚Üí   def get_user(user_id: int) -> Optional[User]: ...
    91‚Üí   if isinstance(value, str): ...  # type narrowing
    92‚Üí   ```
    93‚Üí
    94‚Üí4. For stubborn errors: add `# type: ignore[error-code]` with explanation, or ask user for guidance
    95‚Üí5. Re-run `ty` after each batch
    96‚Üí6. Iterate until passing or errors are documented/suppressed
    97‚Üí
    98‚Üí### 5. Configuration
    99‚Üí
   100‚ÜíIf no config exists, suggest creating `pyproject.toml`:
   101‚Üí
   102‚Üí```toml
   103‚Üí[tool.ruff]
   104‚Üíline-length = 88  
   105‚Üítarget-version = "py311"
   106‚Üí
   107‚Üí[tool.ruff.lint]
   108‚Üíselect = ["E", "F", "I", "N", "W", "UP"]
   109‚Üíignore = ["E501"]
   110‚Üí```
   111‚Üí
   112‚Üí### 6. Edge Cases
   113‚Üí
   114‚Üí- **Third-party code**: Skip vendor directories
   115‚Üí- **Generated code**: Add `# ruff: noqa` or type ignore comments
   116‚Üí- **Test files**: May need different rules
   117‚Üí- **Legacy code**: Consider incremental adoption (one module at a time)
   118‚Üí- **Conflicting fixes**: Revert and document if functionality breaks
   119‚Üí
   120‚Üí### 7. Final Verification
   121‚Üí
   122‚Üí```bash
   123‚Üíruff check /path/to/scope
   124‚Üíruff format --check /path/to/scope
   125‚Üíty /path/to/scope
   126‚Üí```
   127‚Üí
   128‚Üí### 8. Summary Report
   129‚Üí
   130‚ÜíProvide:
   131‚Üí- Total files processed
   132‚Üí- Issues fixed (formatting, linting, type errors)
   133‚Üí- Remaining issues and why not fixed
   134‚Üí- Configuration files created/modified
   135‚Üí
   136‚Üí### 9. Best Practices
   137‚Üí
   138‚Üí- Ensure user has committed changes before large fixes
   139‚Üí- For 100+ files, offer incremental processing
   140‚Üí- Explain complex fixes; don't silently change business logic
   141‚Üí- Preserve behavior; ask when uncertain
   142‚Üí- Document suppressions with error codes and reasons
   143‚Üí- Encourage running tests after fixes
   144‚Üí
   145‚Üí### Common Commands
   146‚Üí
   147‚Üí```bash
   148‚Üíruff check <path>              # Check linting
   149‚Üíruff check --fix <path>        # Auto-fix
   150‚Üíruff format <path>             # Format code
   151‚Üíruff format --check <path>     # Check formatting
   152‚Üíty <path>                      # Type check
   153‚Üí
   154‚Üí# Combined
   155‚Üíruff format . && ruff check --fix . && ty .
   156‚Üí```
   157‚Üí
   158‚Üí### Error Handling
   159‚Üí
   160‚Üí- If configuration has syntax errors, fix them first
   161‚Üí- If fixes cause test failures, revert and explain
   162‚Üí- If Python version incompatible, adjust target-version in config
   163‚Üí- For permission errors, suggest appropriate permissions or adjust scope

1‚Üí---
     2‚Üíname: code-onboarder
     3‚Üídescription: Generate comprehensive technical documentation for Python codebases. Use when analyzing a repository to create an architectural overview for experienced engineers, covering system design, core abstractions, data models, and component relationships.
     4‚Üí---
     5‚Üí
     6‚Üí# Codebase Documentation Generator
     7‚Üí
     8‚ÜíThis skill guides you through creating high-quality technical documentation that describes a Python codebase's architecture and design to an experienced engineer.
     9‚Üí
    10‚Üí## When to Use
    11‚Üí
    12‚Üí- Use this skill when analyzing a Python repository to create architectural documentation
    13‚Üí- When an engineer needs to understand a codebase's high-level structure and design
    14‚Üí- When onboarding senior engineers to a new project
    15‚Üí- When documenting system architecture for technical design reviews
    16‚Üí- When creating reference documentation that explains "how the system works" rather than "how to use it"
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí## Instructions
    19‚Üí
    20‚Üí### 1. Initial Repository Analysis
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíFirst, explore the repository structure to understand its organization:
    23‚Üí```bash
    24‚Üí# Get directory structure
    25‚Üífind /path/to/repo -type f -name "*.py" | head -50
    26‚Üítree -L 3 /path/to/repo  # if available
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí# Identify key files
    29‚Üíls -la /path/to/repo
    30‚Üícat /path/to/repo/README.md
    31‚Üícat /path/to/repo/setup.py  # or pyproject.toml
    32‚Üí```
    33‚Üí
    34‚ÜíLook for:
    35‚Üí- Entry points (main.py, __main__.py, CLI scripts)
    36‚Üí- Configuration files (config.py, settings.py, .env files)
    37‚Üí- Core modules (usually in src/, lib/, or project name directory)
    38‚Üí- Tests directory structure
    39‚Üí- Dependencies (requirements.txt, pyproject.toml, Pipfile)
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí### 2. Identify System Architecture Patterns
    42‚Üí
    43‚ÜíDetermine the architectural style:
    44‚Üí- **Layered architecture**: Presentation, business logic, data access layers
    45‚Üí- **Microservices**: Multiple independent services
    46‚Üí- **Event-driven**: Message queues, pub/sub patterns
    47‚Üí- **Plugin-based**: Extensible core with plugins
    48‚Üí- **MVC/MVT**: Model-View-Controller patterns
    49‚Üí- **Pipeline/ETL**: Data processing workflows
    50‚Üí- **Client-Server**: API servers, background workers
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí### 3. Map Core Components
    53‚Üí
    54‚ÜíIdentify and categorize major components:
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üí**Core Abstractions**:
    57‚Üí- Base classes and interfaces
    58‚Üí- Abstract base classes (ABCs)
    59‚Üí- Protocol classes (typing.Protocol)
    60‚Üí- Key design patterns (Factory, Strategy, Observer, etc.)
    61‚Üí
    62‚Üí**Data Models**:
    63‚Üí- Domain entities (dataclasses, Pydantic models, ORM models)
    64‚Üí- DTOs (Data Transfer Objects)
    65‚Üí- Configuration structures
    66‚Üí- State representations
    67‚Üí
    68‚Üí**Business Logic**:
    69‚Üí- Services and managers
    70‚Üí- Processors and handlers
    71‚Üí- Validators and transformers
    72‚Üí- Domain-specific algorithms
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üí**Infrastructure**:
    75‚Üí- Database access layers
    76‚Üí- API clients and integrations
    77‚Üí- Caching mechanisms
    78‚Üí- Message queue interfaces
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí### 4. Create System Architecture Diagram
    81‚Üí
    82‚ÜíUse Mermaid to visualize the system. Choose the appropriate diagram type:
    83‚Üí
    84‚Üí**For layered architectures**:
    85‚Üí```mermaid
    86‚Üígraph TB
    87‚Üí    subgraph "Presentation Layer"
    88‚Üí        API[REST API]
    89‚Üí        CLI[CLI Interface]
    90‚Üí    end
    91‚Üí    
    92‚Üí    subgraph "Business Logic Layer"
    93‚Üí        Service[Service Layer]
    94‚Üí        Domain[Domain Models]
    95‚Üí    end
    96‚Üí    
    97‚Üí    subgraph "Data Access Layer"
    98‚Üí        Repo[Repository]
    99‚Üí        ORM[ORM Models]
   100‚Üí    end
   101‚Üí    
   102‚Üí    API --> Service
   103‚Üí    CLI --> Service
   104‚Üí    Service --> Domain
   105‚Üí    Service --> Repo
   106‚Üí    Repo --> ORM
   107‚Üí```
   108‚Üí
   109‚Üí**For component-based systems**:
   110‚Üí```mermaid
   111‚Üígraph LR
   112‚Üí    Client[Client] --> Gateway[API Gateway]
   113‚Üí    Gateway --> Auth[Auth Service]
   114‚Üí    Gateway --> Core[Core Service]
   115‚Üí    Core --> Cache[(Cache)]
   116‚Üí    Core --> DB[(Database)]
   117‚Üí    Core --> Queue[Message Queue]
   118‚Üí    Worker[Background Worker] --> Queue
   119‚Üí```
   120‚Üí
   121‚Üí**For data flow pipelines**:
   122‚Üí```mermaid
   123‚Üíflowchart LR
   124‚Üí    Input[Data Input] --> Extract[Extractor]
   125‚Üí    Extract --> Transform[Transformer]
   126‚Üí    Transform --> Validate[Validator]
   127‚Üí    Validate --> Load[Loader]
   128‚Üí    Load --> Output[(Data Store)]
   129‚Üí```
   130‚Üí
   131‚Üí### 5. Document Structure
   132‚Üí
   133‚ÜíCreate a markdown document with the following sections:
   134‚Üí
   135‚Üí#### Required Sections:
   136‚Üí
   137‚Üí**1. Overview** (2-3 paragraphs)
   138‚Üí- What the system does (purpose and domain)
   139‚Üí- Who it's for (target users/use cases)
   140‚Üí- Key capabilities and constraints
   141‚Üí
   142‚Üí**2. System Architecture** (with Mermaid diagram)
   143‚Üí- High-level component diagram
   144‚Üí- Architecture pattern explanation
   145‚Üí- Major subsystems and their responsibilities
   146‚Üí- Communication patterns between components
   147‚Üí
   148‚Üí**3. Core Concepts & Abstractions**
   149‚Üí- Fundamental abstractions that define the system
   150‚Üí- Key interfaces and base classes
   151‚Üí- Design patterns employed
   152‚Üí- Domain-specific terminology
   153‚Üí
   154‚Üí**4. Data Models**
   155‚Üí- Primary entities and their relationships
   156‚Üí- Data flow through the system
   157‚Üí- State management approach
   158‚Üí- Persistence strategy
   159‚Üí
   160‚Üí**5. Component Deep-Dive**
   161‚ÜíFor each major component:
   162‚Üí- Purpose and responsibility
   163‚Üí- Key classes/modules
   164‚Üí- Dependencies and relationships
   165‚Üí- Entry points and public interfaces
   166‚Üí
   167‚Üí**6. Technical Details**
   168‚Üí- Technology stack (frameworks, libraries)
   169‚Üí- External dependencies
   170‚Üí- Configuration and environment
   171‚Üí- Build and deployment considerations
   172‚Üí
   173‚Üí#### Optional Sections (include if relevant):
   174‚Üí
   175‚Üí**7. Key Workflows**
   176‚Üí- Critical user journeys or data flows
   177‚Üí- Step-by-step sequence for important operations
   178‚Üí
   179‚Üí**8. Extension Points**
   180‚Üí- How to extend or customize the system
   181‚Üí- Plugin mechanisms or hooks
   182‚Üí- Configuration options
   183‚Üí
   184‚Üí**9. Design Decisions**
   185‚Üí- Important architectural choices
   186‚Üí- Tradeoffs made
   187‚Üí- Rationale for key patterns
   188‚Üí
   189‚Üí### 6. Writing Guidelines
   190‚Üí
   191‚Üí**Tone & Audience**:
   192‚Üí- Write for staff/principal engineers who understand software architecture
   193‚Üí- Assume knowledge of design patterns, architectural styles, and best practices
   194‚Üí- Be concise but comprehensive‚Äîavoid over-explaining basics
   195‚Üí- Use precise technical terminology
   196‚Üí
   197‚Üí**Content Principles**:
   198‚Üí- Focus on "why" and "how it works", not "how to use it"
   199‚Üí- Explain the mental model an engineer needs to reason about the system
   200‚Üí- Highlight non-obvious design decisions and patterns
   201‚Üí- Call out important constraints or invariants
   202‚Üí- Avoid tutorial-style step-by-step instructions
   203‚Üí
   204‚Üí**Code References**:
   205‚Üí- Reference specific files/classes when illustrating concepts
   206‚Üí- Use short, relevant code snippets (5-10 lines max)
   207‚Üí- Format code blocks with Python syntax highlighting
   208‚Üí- Include file paths: `src/services/auth.py`
   209‚Üí
   210‚Üí**Clarity**:
   211‚Üí- Use headers and subheaders for scanability
   212‚Üí- Keep paragraphs focused (3-5 sentences)
   213‚Üí- Use bullet points for lists of items
   214‚Üí- Bold key terms on first usage
   215‚Üí- Include Mermaid diagrams for visual clarity
   216‚Üí
   217‚Üí### 7. Analysis Process
   218‚Üí
   219‚ÜíFollow this systematic approach:
   220‚Üí
   221‚Üí1. **Start at the entry points**: Trace from main.py, CLI commands, or API routes
   222‚Üí2. **Follow the imports**: Build a dependency graph of modules
   223‚Üí3. **Identify layers**: Group files by their architectural role
   224‚Üí4. **Find the core**: Locate domain models and business logic
   225‚Üí5. **Map data flow**: Track how data moves through the system
   226‚Üí6. **Document patterns**: Note recurring design patterns
   227‚Üí7. **Capture dependencies**: List frameworks, libraries, external services
   228‚Üí
   229‚Üí### 8. Quality Checklist
   230‚Üí
   231‚ÜíBefore finalizing, ensure the documentation:
   232‚Üí
   233‚Üí- [ ] Has a clear, accurate Mermaid architecture diagram
   234‚Üí- [ ] Explains the system's purpose and domain
   235‚Üí- [ ] Identifies all major components and their responsibilities
   236‚Üí- [ ] Describes core abstractions and their relationships
   237‚Üí- [ ] Documents data models and their lifecycle
   238‚Üí- [ ] References specific files/classes for key components
   239‚Üí- [ ] Explains how components communicate
   240‚Üí- [ ] Notes important design decisions or patterns
   241‚Üí- [ ] Is scannable with clear headers and structure
   242‚Üí- [ ] Uses appropriate technical depth for the audience
   243‚Üí- [ ] Has accurate file paths and code references
   244‚Üí
   245‚Üí### 9. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
   246‚Üí
   247‚Üí- **Don't write user documentation**: Focus on architecture, not usage
   248‚Üí- **Don't exhaustively list every file**: Highlight key components only
   249‚Üí- **Don't explain basic concepts**: Assume engineering expertise
   250‚Üí- **Don't use vague descriptions**: Be specific about responsibilities
   251‚Üí- **Don't ignore the big picture**: Start with architecture before details
   252‚Üí- **Don't create overly complex diagrams**: Keep diagrams focused and readable
   253‚Üí- **Don't forget the "why"**: Explain rationale for design choices
   254‚Üí
   255‚Üí### 10. Example Output Structure
   256‚Üí```markdown
   257‚Üí# [Project Name] Technical Architecture
   258‚Üí
   259‚Üí## Overview
   260‚Üí[2-3 paragraph description of system purpose, capabilities, and context]
   261‚Üí
   262‚Üí## System Architecture
   263‚Üí
   264‚Üí[Mermaid diagram showing high-level architecture]
   265‚Üí
   266‚Üí[1-2 paragraphs explaining the architecture pattern and design philosophy]
   267‚Üí
   268‚Üí## Core Concepts
   269‚Üí
   270‚Üí### [Concept 1]
   271‚Üí[Description with code references]
   272‚Üí
   273‚Üí### [Concept 2]
   274‚Üí[Description with code references]
   275‚Üí
   276‚Üí## Data Models
   277‚Üí
   278‚Üí### [Entity 1]
   279‚Üí[Description, relationships, lifecycle]
   280‚Üí
   281‚Üí### [Entity 2]
   282‚Üí[Description, relationships, lifecycle]
   283‚Üí
   284‚Üí## Component Breakdown
   285‚Üí
   286‚Üí### [Component 1]: [Name]
   287‚Üí**Purpose**: [What it does]
   288‚Üí**Location**: `path/to/component`
   289‚Üí**Key Classes**: 
   290‚Üí- `ClassName1`: [Brief description]
   291‚Üí- `ClassName2`: [Brief description]
   292‚Üí
   293‚Üí**Dependencies**: [What it depends on]
   294‚Üí**Used By**: [What depends on it]
   295‚Üí
   296‚Üí[Additional details as needed]
   297‚Üí
   298‚Üí## Technical Stack
   299‚Üí- **Framework**: [e.g., FastAPI, Django, Flask]
   300‚Üí- **Database**: [e.g., PostgreSQL, MongoDB]
   301‚Üí- **Key Libraries**: [Important dependencies]
   302‚Üí
   303‚Üí## Key Design Decisions
   304‚Üí- [Decision 1 and rationale]
   305‚Üí- [Decision 2 and rationale]
   306‚Üí```
   307‚Üí
   308‚Üí### 11. Interaction with User
   309‚Üí
   310‚ÜíIf you need clarification:
   311‚Üí
   312‚Üí- **Ask about scope**: "Should I focus on the core engine or include deployment infrastructure?"
   313‚Üí- **Clarify audience**: "Are we documenting for engineers joining the team or for external review?"
   314‚Üí- **Verify depth**: "Should I include detailed sequence diagrams for key workflows?"
   315‚Üí- **Check focus areas**: "Are there specific components you want emphasized?"
   316‚Üí
   317‚ÜíUse the conversation naturally‚Äîdon't ask all questions upfront, but clarify as needed while analyzing.
   318‚Üí
   319‚Üí## Output Location
   320‚Üí
   321‚ÜíSave the final documentation  `system-architecture.md` in the root directory of the project.

     2‚Üíname: pr-description
     3‚Üídescription: Analyze git diff between current branch and a reference branch (main/staging), and generate a well-structured, reviewer-friendly PR description in markdown format.
     4‚Üí---
     5‚Üí
     6‚Üí# PR Description Generator
     7‚Üí
     8‚ÜíAnalyze changes between the current branch and a reference branch, then generate a coherent, well-formatted pull request description that highlights important changes and guides reviewers.
     9‚Üí
    10‚Üí## When to Use
    11‚Üí
    12‚Üí- User asks to write or generate a PR description
    13‚Üí- User wants to summarize branch changes for a pull request
    14‚Üí- User is preparing to merge a feature branch into main/staging
    15‚Üí- User asks "what changed in this branch?" with intent to create a PR
    16‚Üí- User mentions "PR", "pull request", "merge request", or "code review"
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí## Instructions
    19‚Üí
    20‚Üí### 1. Clarify Parameters
    21‚Üí
    22‚ÜíDetermine:
    23‚Üí- **Reference branch**: Which branch to compare against? (default: `main`, common alternatives: `staging`, `develop`)
    24‚Üí- **Current branch**: Confirm the current branch name
    25‚Üí- **PR type**: Feature, bugfix, refactor, chore, or mixed?
    26‚Üí- **Target audience**: Internal team, open source, or specific reviewers?
    27‚Üí
    28‚ÜíIf the reference branch is unclear, ask the user before proceeding.
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí### 2. Gather Branch Context
    31‚Üí
    32‚ÜíGet branch information:
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí```bash
    35‚Üí# Current branch name
    36‚Üígit branch --show-current
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí# Ensure we have latest refs
    39‚Üígit fetch origin
    40‚Üí
    41‚Üí# Check if reference branch exists
    42‚Üígit rev-parse --verify origin/main 2>/dev/null || git rev-parse --verify main
    43‚Üí```
    44‚Üí
    45‚Üí### 3. Analyze the Diff
    46‚Üí
    47‚Üí#### Step A: Get High-Level Statistics
    48‚Üí
    49‚Üí```bash
    50‚Üí# Summary of changes
    51‚Üígit diff origin/<reference-branch>...HEAD --stat
    52‚Üí
    53‚Üí# Number of commits
    54‚Üígit rev-list --count origin/<reference-branch>..HEAD
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üí# List of commits (for context)
    57‚Üígit log origin/<reference-branch>..HEAD --oneline --no-merges
    58‚Üí```
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí#### Step B: Identify Changed Files by Category
    61‚Üí
    62‚Üí```bash
    63‚Üí# All changed files
    64‚Üígit diff origin/<reference-branch>...HEAD --name-only
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí# Files by change type
    67‚Üígit diff origin/<reference-branch>...HEAD --name-status
    68‚Üí```
    69‚Üí
    70‚ÜíCategorize changes into:
    71‚Üí- **Core logic**: Source files with business logic changes
    72‚Üí- **Tests**: New or modified test files
    73‚Üí- **Configuration**: Config files, CI/CD, build scripts
    74‚Üí- **Documentation**: README, docs, comments
    75‚Üí- **Dependencies**: package.json, requirements.txt, etc.
    76‚Üí
    77‚Üí#### Step C: Analyze Key Changes
    78‚Üí
    79‚ÜíFor significant files (large diffs or critical paths):
    80‚Üí
    81‚Üí```bash
    82‚Üí# View specific file diff
    83‚Üígit diff origin/<reference-branch>...HEAD -- <file-path>
    84‚Üí
    85‚Üí# Show function-level changes (if supported)
    86‚Üígit diff origin/<reference-branch>...HEAD --function-context -- <file-path>
    87‚Üí```
    88‚Üí
    89‚ÜíFocus on:
    90‚Üí- New files (especially new modules/classes)
    91‚Üí- Deleted files (breaking changes?)
    92‚Üí- Files with large line changes
    93‚Üí- Files in critical paths (auth, payments, core business logic)
    94‚Üí
    95‚Üí### 4. Identify Key Themes
    96‚Üí
    97‚ÜíGroup changes into coherent themes:
    98‚Üí
    99‚Üí1. **Primary feature/fix**: What's the main purpose of this PR?
   100‚Üí2. **Supporting changes**: What else was modified to support the main change?
   101‚Üí3. **Refactoring**: Any code improvements made along the way?
   102‚Üí4. **Cleanup**: Dead code removal, formatting, minor fixes?
   103‚Üí
   104‚Üí### 5. Generate PR Description
   105‚Üí
   106‚ÜíStructure the description using this template:
   107‚Üí
   108‚Üí```markdown
   109‚Üí## Summary
   110‚Üí
   111‚Üí[1-3 sentences describing WHAT this PR does and WHY]
   112‚Üí
   113‚Üí## Key Changes
   114‚Üí
   115‚Üí### [Theme 1: e.g., "New Authentication Flow"]
   116‚Üí- [Bullet point describing specific change]
   117‚Üí- [Another change in this theme]
   118‚Üí
   119‚Üí### [Theme 2: e.g., "Database Schema Updates"]
   120‚Üí- [Changes in this area]
   121‚Üí
   122‚Üí## Files Changed
   123‚Üí
   124‚Üí| Category | Files | Purpose |
   125‚Üí|----------|-------|---------|
   126‚Üí| Core | `path/to/file.py` | [Brief description] |
   127‚Üí| Tests | `tests/test_*.py` | [What's tested] |
   128‚Üí| Config | `config.yaml` | [What changed] |
   129‚Üí
   130‚Üí## Review Guide
   131‚Üí
   132‚Üí### What to Focus On
   133‚Üí- [Specific file or section that needs careful review]
   134‚Üí- [Any complex logic worth double-checking]
   135‚Üí- [Potential edge cases to consider]
   136‚Üí
   137‚Üí### What's Safe to Skim
   138‚Üí- [Straightforward changes]
   139‚Üí- [Auto-generated or boilerplate changes]
   140‚Üí
   141‚Üí## Testing
   142‚Üí
   143‚Üí- [ ] [How this was tested]
   144‚Üí- [ ] [Any manual testing steps]
   145‚Üí
   146‚Üí## Breaking Changes
   147‚Üí
   148‚Üí[List any breaking changes, or "None" if backward compatible]
   149‚Üí
   150‚Üí## Related
   151‚Üí
   152‚Üí- Related issue: #[number] (if applicable)
   153‚Üí- Related PR: #[number] (if applicable)
   154‚Üí- Documentation: [link] (if applicable)
   155‚Üí```
   156‚Üí
   157‚Üí### 6. Writing Guidelines
   158‚Üí
   159‚Üí**DO:**
   160‚Üí- Lead with the "why" - what problem does this solve?
   161‚Üí- Group related changes together
   162‚Üí- Highlight files/sections needing careful review
   163‚Üí- Mention potential risks or areas of concern
   164‚Üí- Include testing evidence
   165‚Üí- Keep the summary under 3 sentences
   166‚Üí- Write the output as a markdown file in the project root
   167‚Üí
   168‚Üí**DON'T:**
   169‚Üí- List every single changed line
   170‚Üí- Include implementation details in the summary
   171‚Üí- Bury important information in long paragraphs
   172‚Üí- Assume the reviewer knows the context
   173‚Üí- Skip the "why" and only describe the "what"
   174‚Üí
   175‚Üí### 7. Reviewer-Friendly Tips
   176‚Üí
   177‚ÜíTo make the reviewer's job easier:
   178‚Üí
   179‚Üí1. **Point out the "scary" parts**: Identify changes that touch critical systems
   180‚Üí2. **Explain non-obvious decisions**: Why was X done this way instead of Y?
   181‚Üí3. **Call out test coverage**: What's tested, what's not, and why?
   182‚Üí4. **Flag dependencies**: Does this PR depend on or block other work?
   183‚Üí5. **Suggest review order**: "Start with X, then Y makes more sense"
   184‚Üí
   185‚Üí### 8. Edge Cases
   186‚Üí
   187‚Üí- **Large PRs (50+ files)**: Suggest breaking into smaller PRs, or provide a detailed roadmap
   188‚Üí- **Refactoring + Feature**: Clearly separate what's refactoring vs. new functionality
   189‚Üí- **Generated code**: Call out auto-generated files that don't need review
   190‚Üí- **Merge conflicts**: Note if the branch needs rebasing
   191‚Üí- **Draft PRs**: Indicate what's still WIP and what's ready for feedback
   192‚Üí
   193‚Üí### 9. Final Verification
   194‚Üí
   195‚ÜíBefore presenting the description:
   196‚Üí
   197‚Üí```bash
   198‚Üí# Verify diff is accurate
   199‚Üígit diff origin/<reference-branch>...HEAD --stat | head -20
   200‚Üí
   201‚Üí# Check for uncommitted changes that might be missing
   202‚Üígit status
   203‚Üí```
   204‚Üí
   205‚ÜíConfirm with user:
   206‚Üí- Does the summary capture the intent?
   207‚Üí- Are all major changes mentioned?
   208‚Üí- Is anything sensitive that shouldn't be in the PR description?
   209‚Üí
   210‚Üí### Common Commands
   211‚Üí
   212‚Üí```bash
   213‚Üí# Compare against main
   214‚Üígit diff origin/main...HEAD --stat
   215‚Üígit log origin/main..HEAD --oneline
   216‚Üí
   217‚Üí# Compare against staging
   218‚Üígit diff origin/staging...HEAD --stat
   219‚Üígit log origin/staging..HEAD --oneline
   220‚Üí
   221‚Üí# See what files changed
   222‚Üígit diff origin/main...HEAD --name-only
   223‚Üí
   224‚Üí# See specific file changes
   225‚Üígit diff origin/main...HEAD -- path/to/file.py
   226‚Üí
   227‚Üí# Get commit messages (useful for PR description)
   228‚Üígit log origin/main..HEAD --pretty=format:"- %s"
   229‚Üí```
   230‚Üí
   231‚Üí### Error Handling
   232‚Üí
   233‚Üí- **Branch doesn't exist**: Suggest alternatives (`main` vs `master`, `staging` vs `develop`)
   234‚Üí- **No changes found**: Verify the comparison branches; branch may already be merged
   235‚Üí- **Uncommitted changes**: Warn user and ask if they should be included
   236‚Üí- **Remote not fetched**: Run `git fetch` and retry
   237‚Üí- **Detached HEAD**: Identify the actual branch or commit being compared
   238‚Üí

1‚Üí--- 2‚Üíname: visual-explainer 3‚Üídescription: Generate beautiful, self-contained HTML pages that visually explain systems, code changes, plans, and data. Use when the user asks for a diagram, architecture overview, diff review, plan review, project recap, comparison table, or any visual explanation of technical concepts. Also use proactively when you are about to render a complex ASCII table (4+ rows or 3+ columns) ‚Äî present it as a styled HTML page instead. 4‚Üílicense: MIT 5‚Üícompatibility: Requires a browser to view generated HTML files. Optional surf-cli for AI image generation. 6‚Üímetadata: 7‚Üí author: nicobailon 8‚Üí version: "0.4.4" 9‚Üí--- 10‚Üí 11‚Üí# Visual Explainer 12‚Üí 13‚ÜíGenerate self-contained HTML files for technical diagrams, visualizations, and data tables. Always open the result in the browser. Never fall back to ASCII art when this skill is loaded. 14‚Üí 15‚Üí**Proactive table rendering.** When you're about to present tabular data as an ASCII box-drawing table in the terminal (comparisons, audits, feature matrices, status reports, any structured rows/columns), generate an HTML page instead. The threshold: if the table has 4+ rows or 3+ columns, it belongs in the browser. Don't wait for the user to ask ‚Äî render it as HTML automatically and tell them the file path. You can still include a brief text summary in the chat, but the table itself should be the HTML page. 16‚Üí 17‚Üí## Workflow 18‚Üí 19‚Üí### 1. Think (5 seconds, not 5 minutes) 20‚Üí 21‚ÜíBefore writing HTML, commit to a direction. Don't default to "dark theme with blue accents" every time. 22‚Üí 23‚Üí**Visual is always default.** Even essays, blog posts, and articles get visual treatment ‚Äî extract structure into cards, diagrams, grids, tables. 24‚Üí 25‚ÜíProse patterns (lead paragraphs, pull quotes, callout boxes) are **accent elements** within visual pages, not a separate mode. Use them to highlight key points or provide breathing room, but the page structure remains visual. 26‚Üí 27‚ÜíFor prose accents, see "Prose Page Elements" in `./references/css-patterns.md`. For everything else, use the standard freeform approach with aesthetic directions below. 28‚Üí 29‚Üí**Who is looking?** A developer understanding a system? A PM seeing the big picture? A team reviewing a proposal? This shapes information density and visual complexity. 30‚Üí 31‚Üí**What type of content?** Architecture, flowchart, sequence, data flow, schema/ER, state machine, mind map, data table, timeline, dashboard, or prose-first page. Each has distinct layout needs and rendering approaches (see Diagram Types below). 32‚Üí 33‚Üí**What aesthetic?** Pick one and commit. The constrained aesthetics (Blueprint, Editorial, Paper/ink) are safer ‚Äî they have specific requirements that prevent generic output. The flexible ones (IDE-inspired) require more discipline. 34‚Üí 35‚Üí**Constrained aesthetics (prefer these):** 36‚Üí- Blueprint (technical drawing feel, subtle grid background, deep slate/blue palette, monospace labels, precise borders) ‚Äî see `websocket-implementation-plan.html` for reference 37‚Üí- Editorial (serif headlines like Instrument Serif or Crimson Pro, generous whitespace, muted earth tones or deep navy + gold) 38‚Üí- Paper/ink (warm cream `#faf7f5` background, terracotta/sage accents, informal feel) 39‚Üí- Monochrome terminal (green/amber on near-black, monospace everything, CRT glow optional) 40‚Üí 41‚Üí**Flexible aesthetics (use with caution):** 42‚Üí- IDE-inspired (borrow a real, named color scheme: Dracula, Nord, Catppuccin Mocha/Latte, Solarized Dark/Light, Gruvbox, One Dark, Ros√© Pine) ‚Äî commit to the actual palette, don't approximate 43‚Üí- Data-dense (small type, tight spacing, maximum information, muted colors) 44‚Üí 45‚Üí**Explicitly forbidden:** 46‚Üí- Neon dashboard (cyan + magenta + purple on dark) ‚Äî always produces AI slop 47‚Üí- Gradient mesh (pink/purple/cyan blobs) ‚Äî too generic 48‚Üí- Any combination of Inter font + violet/indigo accents + gradient text 49‚Üí 50‚ÜíVary the choice each time. If the last diagram was dark and technical, make the next one light and editorial. The swap test: if you replaced your styling with a generic dark theme and nobody would notice the difference, you haven't designed anything. 51‚Üí 52‚Üí### 2. Structure 53‚Üí 54‚Üí**Read the reference material** before generating. Don't memorize it ‚Äî read it each time to absorb the patterns. 55‚Üí- For text-heavy architecture overviews (card content matters more than topology): read `./templates/architecture.html` 56‚Üí- For flowcharts, sequence diagrams, ER, state machines, mind maps: read `./templates/mermaid-flowchart.html` 57‚Üí- For data tables, comparisons, audits, feature matrices: read `./templates/data-table.html` 58‚Üí- For slide deck presentations (when `--slides` flag is present or `/generate-slides` is invoked): read `./templates/slide-deck.html` and `./references/slide-patterns.md` 59‚Üí- For prose-heavy publishable pages (READMEs, articles, blog posts, essays): read the "Prose Page Elements" section in `./references/css-patterns.md` and "Typography by Content Voice" in `./references/libraries.md` 60‚Üí 61‚Üí**For CSS/layout patterns and SVG connectors**, read `./references/css-patterns.md`. 62‚Üí 63‚Üí**For pages with 4+ sections** (reviews, recaps, dashboards), also read `./references/responsive-nav.md` for section navigation with sticky sidebar TOC on desktop and horizontal scrollable bar on mobile. 64‚Üí 65‚Üí**Choosing a rendering approach:** 66‚Üí 67‚Üí| Content type | Approach | Why | 68‚Üí|---|---|---| 69‚Üí| Architecture (text-heavy) | CSS Grid cards + flow arrows | Rich card content (descriptions, code, tool lists) needs CSS control | 70‚Üí| Architecture (topology-focused) | **Mermaid** | Visible connections between components need automatic edge routing | 71‚Üí| Flowchart / pipeline | **Mermaid** | Automatic node positioning and edge routing | 72‚Üí| Sequence diagram | **Mermaid** | Lifelines, messages, and activation boxes need automatic layout | 73‚Üí| Data flow | **Mermaid** with edge labels | Connections and data descriptions need automatic edge routing | 74‚Üí| ER / schema diagram | **Mermaid** | Relationship lines between many entities need auto-routing | 75‚Üí| State machine | **Mermaid** | State transitions with labeled edges need automatic layout | 76‚Üí| Mind map | **Mermaid** | Hierarchical branching needs automatic positioning | 77‚Üí| Data table | HTML `` | Semantic markup, accessibility, copy-paste behavior | 78‚Üí| Timeline | CSS (central line + cards) | Simple linear layout doesn't need a layout engine | 79‚Üí| Dashboard | CSS Grid + Chart.js | Card grid with embedded charts | 80‚Üí 81‚Üí**Mermaid theming:** Always use `theme: 'base'` with custom `themeVariables` so colors match your page palette. Use `layout: 'elk'` for complex graphs (requires the `@mermaid-js/layout-elk` package ‚Äî see `./references/libraries.md` for the CDN import). Override Mermaid's SVG classes with CSS for pixel-perfect control. See `./references/libraries.md` for full theming guide. 82‚Üí 83‚Üí**Mermaid containers:** Always center Mermaid diagrams with `display: flex; justify-content: center;`. Add zoom controls (+/‚àí/reset) to every `.mermaid-wrap` container. 84‚Üí 85‚Üí**Mermaid scaling:** Diagrams with 10+ nodes render too small by default. For 10-12 nodes, increase `fontSize` in themeVariables to 18-20px and set `INITIAL_ZOOM` to 1.5-1.6. For 15+ elements, don't try to scale ‚Äî use the hybrid pattern instead (simple Mermaid overview + CSS Grid cards). See "Architecture / System Diagrams" below. 86‚Üí 87‚Üí**Mermaid layout direction:** Prefer `flowchart TD` (top-down) over `flowchart LR` (left-to-right) for complex diagrams. LR spreads horizontally and makes labels unreadable when there are many nodes. Use LR only for simple 3-4 node linear flows. See `./references/libraries.md` "Layout Direction: TD vs LR". 88‚Üí 89‚Üí**Mermaid CSS class collision constraint:** Never define `.node` as a page-level CSS class. Mermaid.js uses `.node` internally on SVG `` elements with `transform: translate(x, y)` for positioning. Page-level `.node` styles (hover transforms, box-shadows) leak into diagrams and break layout. Use the namespaced `.ve-card` class for card components instead. The only safe way to style Mermaid's `.node` is scoped under `.mermaid` (e.g., `.mermaid .node rect`). 90‚Üí 91‚Üí**AI-generated illustrations (optional).** If [surf-cli](https://github.com/nicobailon/surf-cli) is available, you can generate images via Gemini and embed them in the page for creative, illustrative, explanatory, educational, or decorative purposes. Check availability with `which surf`. If available: 92‚Üí 93‚Üí```bash 94‚Üí# Generate to a temp file (use --aspect-ratio for control) 95‚Üísurf gemini "descriptive prompt" --generate-image /tmp/ve-img.png --aspect-ratio 16:9 96‚Üí 97‚Üí# Base64 encode for self-containment (macOS) 98‚ÜíIMG=$(base64 -i /tmp/ve-img.png) 99‚Üí# Linux: IMG=$(base64 -w 0 /tmp/ve-img.png) 100‚Üí 101‚Üí# Embed in HTML and clean up 102‚Üí# 103‚Üírm /tmp/ve-img.png 104‚Üí``` 105‚Üí 106‚ÜíSee `./references/css-patterns.md` for image container styles (hero banners, inline illustrations, captions). 107‚Üí 108‚Üí**When to use:** Hero banners that establish the page's visual tone. Conceptual illustrations for abstract systems that Mermaid can't express (physical infrastructure, user journeys, mental models). Educational diagrams that benefit from artistic or photorealistic rendering. Decorative accents that reinforce the aesthetic. 109‚Üí 110‚Üí**When to skip:** Anything Mermaid or CSS handles well. Generic decoration that doesn't convey meaning. Data-heavy pages where images would distract. Always degrade gracefully ‚Äî if surf isn't available, skip images without erroring. The page should stand on its own with CSS and typography alone. 111‚Üí 112‚Üí**Prompt craft:** Match the image to the page's palette and aesthetic direction. Specify the style (3D render, technical illustration, watercolor, isometric, flat vector, etc.) and mention dominant colors from your CSS variables. Use `--aspect-ratio 16:9` for hero banners, `--aspect-ratio 1:1` for inline illustrations. Keep prompts specific ‚Äî "isometric illustration of a message queue with cyan nodes on dark navy background" beats "a diagram of a queue." 113‚Üí 114‚Üí### 3. Style 115‚Üí 116‚ÜíApply these principles to every diagram: 117‚Üí 118‚Üí**Typography is the diagram.** Pick a distinctive font pairing from the list in `./references/libraries.md`. Every page should use a different pairing from recent generations. 119‚Üí 120‚Üí**Forbidden as `--font-body`:** Inter, Roboto, Arial, Helvetica, system-ui alone. These are AI slop signals. 121‚Üí 122‚Üí**Good pairings (use these):** 123‚Üí- DM Sans + Fira Code (technical, precise) 124‚Üí- Instrument Serif + JetBrains Mono (editorial, refined) 125‚Üí- IBM Plex Sans + IBM Plex Mono (reliable, readable) 126‚Üí- Bricolage Grotesque + Fragment Mono (bold, characterful) 127‚Üí- Plus Jakarta Sans + Azeret Mono (rounded, approachable) 128‚Üí 129‚ÜíLoad via `` in ``. Include a system font fallback in the `font-family` stack for offline resilience. 130‚Üí 131‚Üí**Color tells a story.** Use CSS custom properties for the full palette. Define at minimum: `--bg`, `--surface`, `--border`, `--text`, `--text-dim`, and 3-5 accent colors. Each accent should have a full and a dim variant (for backgrounds). Name variables semantically when possible (`--pipeline-step` not `--blue-3`). Support both themes. 132‚Üí 133‚Üí**Forbidden accent colors:** `#8b5cf6` `#7c3aed` `#a78bfa` (indigo/violet), `#d946ef` (fuchsia), the cyan-magenta-pink combination. These are Tailwind defaults that signal zero design intent. 134‚Üí 135‚Üí**Good accent palettes (use these):** 136‚Üí- Terracotta + sage (`#c2410c`, `#65a30d`) ‚Äî warm, earthy 137‚Üí- Teal + slate (`#0891b2`, `#0369a1`) ‚Äî technical, precise 138‚Üí- Rose + cranberry (`#be123c`, `#881337`) ‚Äî editorial, refined 139‚Üí- Amber + emerald (`#d97706`, `#059669`) ‚Äî data-focused 140‚Üí- Deep blue + gold (`#1e3a5f`, `#d4a73a`) ‚Äî premium, sophisticated 141‚Üí 142‚ÜíPut your primary aesthetic in `:root` and the alternate in the media query: 143‚Üí 144‚Üí```css 145‚Üí/* Light-first (editorial, paper/ink, blueprint): */ 146‚Üí:root { /* light values */ } 147‚Üí@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) { :root { /* dark values */ } } 148‚Üí 149‚Üí/* Dark-first (neon, IDE-inspired, terminal): */ 150‚Üí:root { /* dark values */ } 151‚Üí@media (prefers-color-scheme: light) { :root { /* light values */ } } 152‚Üí``` 153‚Üí 154‚Üí**Surfaces whisper, they don't shout.** Build depth through subtle lightness shifts (2-4% between levels), not dramatic color changes. Borders should be low-opacity rgba (`rgba(255,255,255,0.08)` in dark mode, `rgba(0,0,0,0.08)` in light) ‚Äî visible when you look, invisible when you don't. 155‚Üí 156‚Üí**Backgrounds create atmosphere.** Don't use flat solid colors for the page background. Subtle gradients, faint grid patterns via CSS, or gentle radial glows behind focal areas. The background should feel like a space, not a void. 157‚Üí 158‚Üí**Visual weight signals importance.** Not every section deserves equal visual treatment. Executive summaries and key metrics should dominate the viewport on load (larger type, more padding, subtle accent-tinted background zone). Reference sections (file maps, dependency lists, decision logs) should be compact and stay out of the way. Use `
/` for sections that are useful but not primary ‚Äî the collapsible pattern is in `./references/css-patterns.md`. 159‚Üí 160‚Üí**Surface depth creates hierarchy.** Vary card depth to signal what matters. Hero sections get elevated shadows and accent-tinted backgrounds (`ve-card--hero` pattern). Body content stays flat (default `.ve-card`). Code blocks and secondary content feel recessed (`ve-card--recessed`). See the depth tiers in `./references/css-patterns.md`. Don't make everything elevated ‚Äî when everything pops, nothing does. 161‚Üí 162‚Üí**Animation earns its place.** Staggered fade-ins on page load are almost always worth it ‚Äî they guide the eye through the diagram's hierarchy. Mix animation types by role: `fadeUp` for cards, `fadeScale` for KPIs and badges, `drawIn` for SVG connectors, `countUp` for hero numbers. Hover transitions on interactive-feeling elements make the diagram feel alive. Always respect `prefers-reduced-motion`. CSS transitions and keyframes handle most cases. For orchestrated multi-element sequences, anime.js via CDN is available (see `./references/libraries.md`). 163‚Üí 164‚Üí**Forbidden animations:** 165‚Üí- Animated glowing box-shadows (`@keyframes glow { box-shadow: 0 0 20px... }`) ‚Äî this is AI slop 166‚Üí- Pulsing/breathing effects on static content 167‚Üí- Continuous animations that run after page load (except for progress indicators) 168‚Üí 169‚ÜíKeep animations purposeful: entrance reveals, hover feedback, and user-initiated interactions. Nothing should glow or pulse on its own. 170‚Üí 171‚Üí### 4. Deliver 172‚Üí 173‚Üí**Output location:** Write to `~/.agent/diagrams/`. Use a descriptive filename based on content: `modem-architecture.html`, `pipeline-flow.html`, `schema-overview.html`. The directory persists across sessions. 174‚Üí 175‚Üí**Open in browser:** 176‚Üí- macOS: `open ~/.agent/diagrams/filename.html` 177‚Üí- Linux: `xdg-open ~/.agent/diagrams/filename.html` 178‚Üí 179‚Üí**Tell the user** the file path so they can re-open or share it. 180‚Üí 181‚Üí## Diagram Types 182‚Üí 183‚Üí### Architecture / System Diagrams 184‚ÜíThree approaches depending on complexity: 185‚Üí 186‚Üí**Simple topology (under 10 elements):** Use Mermaid. A `graph TD` with custom `themeVariables` produces readable diagrams with automatic edge routing. 187‚Üí 188‚Üí**Text-heavy overviews (under 15 elements):** CSS Grid with explicit row/column placement. Sections as rounded cards with colored borders and monospace labels. Vertical flow arrows between sections. The reference template at `./templates/architecture.html` demonstrates this pattern. Use when cards need descriptions, code references, tool lists, or other rich content that Mermaid nodes can't hold. 189‚Üí 190‚Üí**Complex architectures (15+ elements):** Use the **hybrid pattern** ‚Äî a simple Mermaid overview (5-8 nodes showing module relationships) followed by detailed CSS Grid cards for each module's internals. This gives you visual topology AND readable details. The overview diagram uses module names with `` tags for key function names. The cards below show full function lists with new/modified badges. Never try to cram 15+ elements into a single Mermaid diagram ‚Äî it will render unreadably small even with zoom controls. 191‚Üí 192‚Üí### Flowcharts / Pipelines 193‚Üí**Use Mermaid.** Automatic node positioning and edge routing produces proper diagrams with connecting lines, decision diamonds, and parallel branches ‚Äî dramatically better than CSS flexbox with arrow characters. Prefer `graph TD` (top-down); use `graph LR` only for simple 3-4 node linear flows. Color-code node types with Mermaid's `classDef` or rely on `themeVariables` for automatic styling. 194‚Üí 195‚Üí### Sequence Diagrams 196‚Üí**Use Mermaid.** Lifelines, messages, activation boxes, notes, and loops all need automatic layout. Use Mermaid's `sequenceDiagram` syntax. Style actors and messages via CSS overrides on `.actor`, `.messageText`, `.activation` classes. 197‚Üí 198‚Üí### Data Flow Diagrams 199‚Üí**Use Mermaid.** Data flow diagrams emphasize connections over boxes ‚Äî exactly what Mermaid excels at. Use `graph TD` (or `graph LR` for simple linear flows) with edge labels for data descriptions. Thicker, colored edges for primary flows. Source/sink nodes styled differently from transform nodes via Mermaid's `classDef`. 200‚Üí 201‚Üí### Schema / ER Diagrams 202‚Üí**Use Mermaid.** Relationship lines between entities need automatic routing. Use Mermaid's `erDiagram` syntax with entity attributes. Style via `themeVariables` and CSS overrides on `.er.entityBox` and `.er.relationshipLine`. 203‚Üí 204‚Üí### State Machines / Decision Trees 205‚Üí**Use Mermaid.** Use `stateDiagram-v2` for states with labeled transitions. Supports nested states, forks, joins, and notes. Decision trees can use `graph TD` with diamond decision nodes. 206‚Üí 207‚Üí**`stateDiagram-v2` label caveat:** Transition labels have a strict parser ‚Äî colons, parentheses, `
`, HTML entities, and most special characters cause silent parse failures ("Syntax error in text"). If your labels need any of these (e.g., `cancel()`, `curate: true`, multi-line labels), use `flowchart TD` instead with rounded nodes and quoted edge labels (`|"label text"|`). Flowcharts handle all special characters and support `
` for line breaks. Reserve `stateDiagram-v2` for simple single-word or plain-text labels. 208‚Üí 209‚Üí### Mind Maps / Hierarchical Breakdowns 210‚Üí**Use Mermaid.** Use `mindmap` syntax for hierarchical branching from a root node. Mermaid handles the radial layout automatically. Style with `themeVariables` to control node colors at each depth level. 211‚Üí 212‚Üí### Data Tables / Comparisons / Audits 213‚ÜíUse a real `
` element ‚Äî not CSS Grid pretending to be a table. Tables get accessibility, copy-paste behavior, and column alignment for free. The reference template at `./templates/data-table.html` demonstrates all patterns below. 214‚Üí 215‚Üí**Use proactively.** Any time you'd render an ASCII box-drawing table in the terminal, generate an HTML table instead. This includes: requirement audits (request vs plan), feature comparisons, status reports, configuration matrices, test result summaries, dependency lists, permission tables, API endpoint inventories ‚Äî any structured rows and columns. 216‚Üí 217‚ÜíLayout patterns: 218‚Üí- Sticky `` so headers stay visible when scrolling long tables 219‚Üí- Alternating row backgrounds via `tr:nth-child(even)` (subtle, 2-3% lightness shift) 220‚Üí- First column optionally sticky for wide tables with horizontal scroll 221‚Üí- Responsive wrapper with `overflow-x: auto` for tables wider than the viewport 222‚Üí- Column width hints via `` or `th` widths ‚Äî let text-heavy columns breathe 223‚Üí- Row hover highlight for scanability 224‚Üí 225‚ÜíStatus indicators (use styled `` elements, never emoji): 226‚Üí- Match/pass/yes: colored dot or checkmark with green background 227‚Üí- Gap/fail/no: colored dot or cross with red background 228‚Üí- Partial/warning: amber indicator 229‚Üí- Neutral/info: dim text or muted badge 230‚Üí 231‚ÜíCell content: 232‚Üí- Wrap long text naturally ‚Äî don't truncate or force single-line 233‚Üí- Use `` for technical references within cells 234‚Üí- Secondary detail text in `` with dimmed color 235‚Üí- Keep numeric columns right-aligned with `tabular-nums` 236‚Üí 237‚Üí### Timeline / Roadmap Views 238‚ÜíVertical or horizontal timeline with a central line (CSS pseudo-element). Phase markers as circles on the line. Content cards branching left/right (alternating) or all to one side. Date labels on the line. Color progression from past (muted) to future (vivid). 239‚Üí 240‚Üí### Dashboard / Metrics Overview 241‚ÜíCard grid layout. Hero numbers large and prominent. Sparklines via inline SVG ``. Progress bars via CSS `linear-gradient` on a div. For real charts (bar, line, pie), use **Chart.js via CDN** (see `./references/libraries.md`). KPI cards with trend indicators (up/down arrows, percentage deltas). 242‚Üí 243‚Üí### Implementation Plans 244‚Üí 245‚ÜíFor visualizing implementation plans, extension designs, or feature specifications. The goal is **understanding the approach**, not reading the full source code. 246‚Üí 247‚Üí**Don't dump full files.** Displaying entire source files inline overwhelms the page and defeats the purpose of a visual explanation. Instead: 248‚Üí- Show **file structure with descriptions** ‚Äî list functions/exports with one-line explanations 249‚Üí- Show **key snippets only** ‚Äî the 5-10 lines that illustrate the core logic 250‚Üí- Use **collapsible sections** for full code if truly needed 251‚Üí 252‚Üí**Code blocks require explicit formatting.** Without `white-space: pre-wrap`, code runs together into an unreadable wall. See the "Code Blocks" section in `./references/css-patterns.md` for the correct pattern. 253‚Üí 254‚Üí**Structure for implementation plans:** 255‚Üí1. Overview/purpose (what problem does this solve?) 256‚Üí2. Flow diagram (Mermaid or CSS cards) 257‚Üí3. File structure with descriptions (not full code) 258‚Üí4. Key implementation details (snippets) 259‚Üí5. API/interface summary 260‚Üí6. Usage examples 261‚Üí 262‚Üí### Documentation (READMEs, Library Docs, API References) 263‚Üí 264‚ÜíWhen visualizing documentation, extract structure into visual elements: 265‚Üí 266‚Üí| Content | Visual Treatment | 267‚Üí|---------|------------------| 268‚Üí| Features | Card grid (2-3 columns) | 269‚Üí| Install/setup steps | Numbered cards or vertical flow | 270‚Üí| API endpoints/commands | Table with sticky header | 271‚Üí| Config options | Table | 272‚Üí| Architecture | Mermaid diagram or CSS card layout | 273‚Üí| Comparisons | Side-by-side panels or table | 274‚Üí| Warnings/notes | Callout boxes | 275‚Üí 276‚ÜíDon't just format the prose ‚Äî transform it. A feature list becomes a card grid. Install steps become a numbered flow. An API reference becomes a table. 277‚Üí 278‚Üí### Prose Accent Elements 279‚Üí 280‚ÜíUse these sparingly within visual pages to highlight key points or provide breathing room. See "Prose Page Elements" in `./references/css-patterns.md` for CSS patterns. 281‚Üí 282‚Üí- **Lead paragraph** ‚Äî larger intro text to set context before diving into cards/grids 283‚Üí- **Pull quote** ‚Äî highlight a key insight; one per page maximum 284‚Üí- **Callout box** ‚Äî warnings, tips, important notes 285‚Üí- **Section divider** ‚Äî visual break between major sections 286‚Üí 287‚Üí**When to use:** A visual page explaining an essay might use a lead paragraph for the thesis, then cards for key arguments. A README visualization might use callout boxes for warnings but otherwise stay card/table-focused. 288‚Üí 289‚Üí## Slide Deck Mode 290‚Üí 291‚ÜíAn alternative output format for presenting content as a magazine-quality slide presentation instead of a scrollable page. **Opt-in only** ‚Äî the agent generates slides when the user invokes `/generate-slides`, passes `--slides` to an existing prompt (e.g., `/diff-review --slides`), or explicitly asks for a slide deck. Never auto-select slide format. 292‚Üí 293‚Üí**Before generating slides**, read `./references/slide-patterns.md` (engine CSS, slide types, transitions, nav chrome, presets) and `./templates/slide-deck.html` (reference template showing all 10 types). Also read `./references/css-patterns.md` for shared patterns and `./references/libraries.md` for Mermaid/Chart.js theming. 294‚Üí 295‚Üí**Slides are not pages reformatted.** They're a different medium. Each slide is exactly one viewport tall (100dvh) with no scrolling. Typography is 2‚Äì3√ó larger. Compositions are bolder. The agent composes a narrative arc (impact ‚Üí context ‚Üí deep dive ‚Üí resolution) rather than mechanically paginating the source. 296‚Üí 297‚Üí**Content completeness.** Changing the medium does not mean dropping content. Follow the "Planning a Deck from a Source Document" process in `slide-patterns.md` before writing any HTML: inventory the source, map every item to slides, verify coverage. Every section, decision, data point, specification, and collapsible detail from the source must appear in the deck. If a plan has 7 sections, the deck covers all 7. If there are 6 decisions, present all 6 ‚Äî not the 2 that fit on one slide. Collapsible details in the source become their own slides. Add more slides rather than cutting content. A 22-slide deck that covers everything beats a 13-slide deck that looks polished but is missing 40% of the source. 298‚Üí 299‚Üí**Slide types (10):** Title, Section Divider, Content, Split, Diagram, Dashboard, Table, Code, Quote, Full-Bleed. Each has a defined layout in `slide-patterns.md`. Content that exceeds a slide's density limit splits across multiple slides ‚Äî never scrolls within a slide. 300‚Üí 301‚Üí**Visual richness:** Check `which surf` at the start. If surf-cli is available, generate 2‚Äì4 images (title slide background, full-bleed background, optional content illustrations) before writing HTML ‚Äî see the Proactive Imagery section in `slide-patterns.md` for the workflow. Also use SVG decorative accents, per-slide background gradients, inline sparklines, and small Mermaid diagrams. Visual-first, text-second. 302‚Üí 303‚Üí**Compositional variety:** Consecutive slides must vary spatial approach ‚Äî centered, left-heavy, right-heavy, split, edge-aligned, full-bleed. Three centered slides in a row means push one off-axis. 304‚Üí 305‚Üí**Curated presets:** Four slide-specific presets as starting points (Midnight Editorial, Warm Signal, Terminal Mono, Swiss Clean) plus the existing 8 aesthetic directions adapted for slides. Pick one and commit. See `slide-patterns.md` for preset CSS values. 306‚Üí 307‚Üí**`--slides` flag on existing prompts:** When a user passes `--slides` to `/diff-review`, `/plan-review`, `/project-recap`, or other prompts, the agent gathers data using the prompt's normal data-gathering instructions, then presents the content as a slide deck instead of a scrollable page. The slide version tells the same story with different structure and pacing ‚Äî but the same breadth of coverage. Don't use the slide format as an excuse to summarize or skip sections that the scrollable version would have included. 308‚Üí 309‚Üí## File Structure 310‚Üí 311‚ÜíEvery diagram is a single self-contained `.html` file. No external assets except CDN links (fonts, optional libraries). Structure: 312‚Üí 313‚Üí```html 314‚Üí 315‚Üí 316‚Üí 317‚Üí 318‚Üí 319‚Üí Descriptive Title 320‚Üí 321‚Üí 324‚Üí 325‚Üí 326‚Üí 327‚Üí 328‚Üí 329‚Üí 330‚Üí 331‚Üí``` 332‚Üí 333‚Üí## Quality Checks 334‚Üí 335‚ÜíBefore delivering, verify: 336‚Üí- **The squint test**: Blur your eyes. Can you still perceive hierarchy? Are sections visually distinct? 337‚Üí- **The swap test**: Would replacing your fonts and colors with a generic dark theme make this indistinguishable from a template? If yes, push the aesthetic further. 338‚Üí- **Both themes**: Toggle your OS between light and dark mode. Both should look intentional, not broken. 339‚Üí- **Information completeness**: Does the diagram actually convey what the user asked for? Pretty but incomplete is a failure. 340‚Üí- **No overflow**: Resize the browser to different widths. No content should clip or escape its container. Every grid and flex child needs `min-width: 0`. Side-by-side panels need `overflow-wrap: break-word`. Never use `display: flex` on `
  • ` for marker characters ‚Äî it creates anonymous flex items that can't shrink, causing lines with many inline `` badges to overflow. Use absolute positioning for markers instead. See the Overflow Protection section in `./references/css-patterns.md`. 341‚Üí- **Mermaid zoom controls**: Every `.mermaid-wrap` container must have zoom controls (+/‚àí/reset buttons), Ctrl/Cmd+scroll zoom, and click-and-drag panning. Complex diagrams render too small without them. The cursor should change to `grab` when zoomed in and `grabbing` while dragging. See `./references/css-patterns.md` for the full pattern. 342‚Üí- **File opens cleanly**: No console errors, no broken font loads, no layout shifts. 343‚Üí 344‚Üí## Anti-Patterns (AI Slop) 345‚Üí 346‚ÜíThese patterns are explicitly forbidden. They signal "AI-generated template" and undermine the skill's purpose of producing distinctive, high-quality diagrams. Review every generated page against this list. 347‚Üí 348‚Üí### Typography 349‚Üí 350‚Üí**Forbidden fonts as primary `--font-body`:** 351‚Üí- Inter ‚Äî the single most overused AI default 352‚Üí- Roboto, Arial, Helvetica ‚Äî generic system fallbacks promoted to primary 353‚Üí- system-ui, sans-serif alone ‚Äî no character, no intent 354‚Üí 355‚Üí**Required:** Pick from the font pairings in `./references/libraries.md`. Every generation should use a different pairing from the last. 356‚Üí 357‚Üí### Color Palette 358‚Üí 359‚Üí**Forbidden accent colors:** 360‚Üí- Indigo-500/violet-500 (`#8b5cf6`, `#7c3aed`, `#a78bfa`) ‚Äî Tailwind's default purple range 361‚Üí- The cyan + magenta + pink neon gradient combination (`#06b6d4` ‚Üí `#d946ef` ‚Üí `#f472b6`) 362‚Üí- Any palette that could be described as "Tailwind defaults with purple/pink/cyan accents" 363‚Üí 364‚Üí**Forbidden color effects:** 365‚Üí- Gradient text on headings (`background: linear-gradient(...); background-clip: text;`) ‚Äî this screams AI-generated 366‚Üí- Animated glowing box-shadows on cards (`box-shadow: 0 0 20px var(--glow); animation: glow 2s...`) 367‚Üí- Multiple overlapping radial glows in accent colors creating a "neon haze" 368‚Üí 369‚Üí**Required:** Build palettes from the reference templates (terracotta/sage, teal/cyan, rose/cranberry, slate/blue) or derive from real IDE themes (Dracula, Nord, Solarized, Gruvbox, Catppuccin). Accents should feel intentional, not default. 370‚Üí 371‚Üí### Section Headers 372‚Üí 373‚Üí**Forbidden:** 374‚Üí- Emoji icons in section headers (üèóÔ∏è, ‚öôÔ∏è, üìÅ, üíª, üìÖ, üîó, ‚ö°, üîß, üì¶, üöÄ, etc.) 375‚Üí- Section headers that all use the same icon-in-rounded-box pattern 376‚Üí 377‚Üí**Required:** Use styled monospace labels with colored dot indicators (see `.section-label` in templates), numbered badges (`section__num` pattern), or asymmetric section dividers. If an icon is genuinely needed, use an inline SVG that matches the palette ‚Äî not emoji. 378‚Üí 379‚Üí### Layout & Hierarchy 380‚Üí 381‚Üí**Forbidden:** 382‚Üí- Perfectly centered everything with uniform padding 383‚Üí- All cards styled identically with the same border-radius, shadow, and spacing 384‚Üí- Every section getting equal visual treatment ‚Äî no hero/primary vs. secondary distinction 385‚Üí- Symmetric layouts where left and right halves mirror each other 386‚Üí 387‚Üí**Required:** Vary visual weight. Hero sections should dominate (larger type, more padding, accent-tinted background). Reference sections should feel compact. Use the depth tiers (hero ‚Üí elevated ‚Üí default ‚Üí recessed). Asymmetric layouts create interest. 388‚Üí 389‚Üí### Template Patterns 390‚Üí 391‚Üí**Forbidden:** 392‚Üí- Three-dot window chrome (red/yellow/green dots) on code blocks ‚Äî this is a clich√© 393‚Üí- KPI cards where every metric has identical gradient text treatment 394‚Üí- "Neon Dashboard" as an aesthetic choice ‚Äî it always produces generic results 395‚Üí- Gradient meshes with pink/purple/cyan blobs in the background 396‚Üí 397‚Üí**Required:** Code blocks use a simple header with filename or language label. KPI cards vary by importance ‚Äî hero numbers for the primary metric, subdued treatment for supporting metrics. Pick aesthetics with natural constraints: Blueprint (must feel technical/precise), Editorial (must have generous whitespace and serif typography), Paper/ink (must feel warm and informal). 398‚Üí 399‚Üí### The Slop Test 400‚Üí 401‚ÜíBefore delivering, apply this test: **Would a developer looking at this page immediately think "AI generated this"?** The telltale signs: 402‚Üí 403‚Üí1. Inter or Roboto font with purple/violet gradient accents 404‚Üí2. Every heading has `background-clip: text` gradient 405‚Üí3. Emoji icons leading every section 406‚Üí4. Glowing cards with animated shadows 407‚Üí5. Cyan-magenta-pink color scheme on dark background 408‚Üí6. Perfectly uniform card grid with no visual hierarchy 409‚Üí7. Three-dot code block chrome 410‚Üí 411‚ÜíIf two or more of these are present, the page is slop. Regenerate with a different aesthetic direction ‚Äî Editorial, Blueprint, Paper/ink, or a specific IDE theme. These constrained aesthetics are harder to mess up because they have specific visual requirements that prevent defaulting to generic patterns. 412‚Üí

     2‚Üíname: "gh-fix-ci"
     3‚Üídescription: "Use when a user asks to debug or fix failing GitHub PR checks that run in GitHub Actions; use `gh` to inspect checks and logs, summarize failure context, draft a fix plan, and implement only after explicit approval. Treat external providers (for example Buildkite) as out of scope and report only the details URL."
     4‚Üí---
     5‚Üí
     6‚Üí
     7‚Üí# Gh Pr Checks Plan Fix
     8‚Üí
     9‚Üí## Overview
    10‚Üí
    11‚ÜíUse gh to locate failing PR checks, fetch GitHub Actions logs for actionable failures, summarize the failure snippet, then propose a fix plan and implement after explicit approval.
    12‚Üí- If a plan-oriented skill (for example `create-plan`) is available, use it; otherwise draft a concise plan inline and request approval before implementing.
    13‚Üí
    14‚ÜíPrereq: authenticate with the standard GitHub CLI once (for example, run `gh auth login`), then confirm with `gh auth status` (repo + workflow scopes are typically required).
    15‚Üí
    16‚Üí## Inputs
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí- `repo`: path inside the repo (default `.`)
    19‚Üí- `pr`: PR number or URL (optional; defaults to current branch PR)
    20‚Üí- `gh` authentication for the repo host
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí## Quick start
    23‚Üí
    24‚Üí- `python "<path-to-skill>/scripts/inspect_pr_checks.py" --repo "." --pr "<number-or-url>"`
    25‚Üí- Add `--json` if you want machine-friendly output for summarization.
    26‚Üí
    27‚Üí## Workflow
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí1. Verify gh authentication.
    30‚Üí   - Run `gh auth status` in the repo.
    31‚Üí   - If unauthenticated, ask the user to run `gh auth login` (ensuring repo + workflow scopes) before proceeding.
    32‚Üí2. Resolve the PR.
    33‚Üí   - Prefer the current branch PR: `gh pr view --json number,url`.
    34‚Üí   - If the user provides a PR number or URL, use that directly.
    35‚Üí3. Inspect failing checks (GitHub Actions only).
    36‚Üí   - Preferred: run the bundled script (handles gh field drift and job-log fallbacks):
    37‚Üí     - `python "<path-to-skill>/scripts/inspect_pr_checks.py" --repo "." --pr "<number-or-url>"`
    38‚Üí     - Add `--json` for machine-friendly output.
    39‚Üí   - Manual fallback:
    40‚Üí     - `gh pr checks <pr> --json name,state,bucket,link,startedAt,completedAt,workflow`
    41‚Üí       - If a field is rejected, rerun with the available fields reported by `gh`.
    42‚Üí     - For each failing check, extract the run id from `detailsUrl` and run:
    43‚Üí       - `gh run view <run_id> --json name,workflowName,conclusion,status,url,event,headBranch,headSha`
    44‚Üí       - `gh run view <run_id> --log`
    45‚Üí     - If the run log says it is still in progress, fetch job logs directly:
    46‚Üí       - `gh api "/repos/<owner>/<repo>/actions/jobs/<job_id>/logs" > "<path>"`
    47‚Üí4. Scope non-GitHub Actions checks.
    48‚Üí   - If `detailsUrl` is not a GitHub Actions run, label it as external and only report the URL.
    49‚Üí   - Do not attempt Buildkite or other providers; keep the workflow lean.
    50‚Üí5. Summarize failures for the user.
    51‚Üí   - Provide the failing check name, run URL (if any), and a concise log snippet.
    52‚Üí   - Call out missing logs explicitly.
    53‚Üí6. Create a plan.
    54‚Üí   - Use the `create-plan` skill to draft a concise plan and request approval.
    55‚Üí7. Implement after approval.
    56‚Üí   - Apply the approved plan, summarize diffs/tests, and ask about opening a PR.
    57‚Üí8. Recheck status.
    58‚Üí   - After changes, suggest re-running the relevant tests and `gh pr checks` to confirm.
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí## Bundled Resources
    61‚Üí
    62‚Üí### scripts/inspect_pr_checks.py
    63‚Üí
    64‚ÜíFetch failing PR checks, pull GitHub Actions logs, and extract a failure snippet. Exits non-zero when failures remain so it can be used in automation.
    65‚Üí
    66‚ÜíUsage examples:
    67‚Üí- `python "<path-to-skill>/scripts/inspect_pr_checks.py" --repo "." --pr "123"`
    68‚Üí- `python "<path-to-skill>/scripts/inspect_pr_checks.py" --repo "." --pr "https://github.com/org/repo/pull/123" --json`
    69‚Üí- `python "<path-to-skill>/scripts/inspect_pr_checks.py" --repo "." --max-lines 200 --context 40`
    70‚Üí
